ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated biryani code; A0FKP (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent node. Variant-specific codes are not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for biryani identified in the international release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of biryani in Singapore is approximately 500 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice with a protein portion (chicken, lamb, fish, or vegetables) and typically served with raita or curry gravy on the side.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Biryani (mixed meat and rice preparation); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for Singapore-adapted portion and sodium values
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides the closest approved-source nutrient profile for biryani as a rice-and-protein mixed dish. Values for the multi-variant (veg/chicken/lamb/fish) record represent a weighted average across variants consistent with the input record. Sodium (240 mg/100 g) and calcium (16 mg/100 g) are cross-referenced against HPB Foodo Singapore data. The input record's per_100g values (energy 150 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 15 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN biryani data and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not reported in the primary source for this composite dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 58); biryani estimated from basmati rice base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for composite biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source — white basmati rice (GI 58, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The protein, fat, and spice components of biryani modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice; GI 58 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 75 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 43.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced ghee and salt during preparation, choose the chicken or fish variant to avoid the higher saturated fat of the lamb variant, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as pickles or extra curry gravy.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 15 g/100 g from basmati rice with GI 58 (medium); a 500 g serving delivers approximately 75 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 43.5, which is high and will produce a significant post-meal glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 150–200 g, increase the protein and vegetable components, choose the fish or vegetable variant to limit saturated fat, and pair with a high-fibre side such as dhal or cucumber raita to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.4 g/100 g from ghee and meat; a 500 g serving delivers 12 g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20 g daily limit recommended for cardiovascular risk management; trans fat is present at 0.1 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the fish or vegetable variant to minimise saturated and trans fat intake, request reduced ghee during preparation, and limit portion size to 300 g to keep saturated fat per meal below 7 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb variant contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; nightshade vegetables (tomato, green chilli) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose the fish variant for its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content, avoid the lamb variant, monitor nightshade sensitivity, and note that turmeric and ginger in the spice blend provide curcumin and gingerols with partial anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Biryani (also spelled Briyani or Biriyani) is a fragrant, layered rice dish of South Asian origin that is widely popular in Singapore as a staple of Indian-Muslim and Indian cuisine. It is prepared using the dum cooking method, in which partially cooked long-grain basmati rice is layered over marinated protein — chicken, lamb/mutton, fish, or mixed vegetables — and slow-cooked in a sealed pot so that the steam from the meat and spices permeates the rice. The spice blend typically includes turmeric, cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, bay leaves, ginger, garlic, and saffron or food colouring, giving the dish its characteristic golden-yellow hue and complex aroma. Yogurt is used in the marinade to tenderise the protein and add a subtle tang, while ghee contributes richness. The finished dish has a fluffy, aromatic rice texture with tender, well-seasoned protein pieces. Nutritionally, a 500 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 30 g fat, and 75 g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index (GI 58) from basmati rice means the dish produces a moderate-to-high glycaemic load at full serving size, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content of 240 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 500 g serving) is a concern for hypertensive individuals. The lamb variant is higher in saturated fat and arachidonic acid, making the fish or vegetable variant preferable for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory dietary goals. Biryani is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant settings and is a common lunch and dinner dish served at Indian-Muslim stalls, wedding banquets, and festive occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plate of biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between chicken biryani and lamb biryani?
How much protein is in biryani?
What is the glycemic index of biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN biryani data (approved primary source for Indian dishes) and cross-referenced with HPB Foodo Singapore data. The multi-variant nature of the dish (veg/chicken/lamb/fish) means the per_100g values represent a weighted average across variants rather than a single measured preparation. GI is estimated from the basmati rice base using the University of Sydney GI Database. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact multi-variant Singapore biryani formulation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this composite multi-variant Singapore biryani preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo databases as of 2024. 2. Per_100g values represent a weighted average across the four variants (veg, chicken, lamb, fish); individual variant values will differ, particularly for protein (higher in lamb/chicken), fat (higher in lamb), and cholesterol (higher in lamb). 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on white basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for composite biryani is available in the approved GI database. 4. The input record listed the nutrient source URL as https://foodo.sg/ but labelled it as HPB Singapore; this has been corrected to reflect both ICMR-NIN and HPB as co-sources. 5. Halal status is based on the cultural/dietary tag in the input record and is consistent with the typical Singapore Indian-Muslim biryani preparation context.
